entries.title: Claude Code enabledPlugins must be a record, not an array
entries.body: Claude Code enabledPlugins in .claude/settings.json must be a record ({"add": true}) not an array (["add"]). Array format causes 'Settings Error: Expected record, but received array' on startup and the entire settings file is skipped.

entries.title: Environment promotion ladder with autoPromote per-env config
entries.body: Agents autonomously climb local → dev → staging when verification passes at each level, with automatic rollback on failure. Production always requires human. autoPromote flag controls the ceiling. Ladder stops on first failure — no retries, log and move on. Two rollback strategies: revert-commit (dev) and redeploy-previous-tag (staging/production).

entries.title: Bare command names in plugin files cause namespace drift
entries.body: Bare command names (/spec) in plugin files caused Claude to recommend /spec instead of /add:spec in consumer projects. Claude reads these files as instructions and reproduces the patterns it sees. Fix: namespace all references to /add:spec format.

entries.title: Away mode needs explicit autonomous operations grant
entries.body: Without explicit grant, agents fall back to 'ask the human' for routine commits/pushes during autonomous sessions. Fix: explicit 'autonomous operations' list (commit, push, create PRs) and 'boundaries' list (no production deploy, no merge to main).

entries.title: Environment boundary is production, not staging
entries.body: Saying 'agents must NOT deploy to staging' is too blunt for Tier 2/3 projects. The real boundary is production. Fix: promotion ladder with autoPromote per-env config, automatic rollback on verification failure.

entries.title: Version bump checklist: 8 locations must be updated together
entries.body: plugin.json, marketplace.json, config.json, README.md, all commands, all skills, reports HTML, website footers. Heading conventions: Commands '# ADD {Name} Command v{X}', Skills '# ADD {Name} Skill v{X}', Rules '# ADD Rule: {Name}' (no version). Historical references to past versions should NOT be bumped.

entries.title: First retro: spec-before-code promoted to Tier 1
entries.body: 5/5 specs marked Complete (4 were Draft despite implementations — spec-after-code violation). Dog-food the methodology or lose credibility. Feedback filtering posture: accept gems from criticism, maintain independent view.

entries.title: Namespace fix: 205 bare references across 30 files
entries.body: Created README.md, SVG infographic, HTML overview report. Website built with raspberry palette. Critical: namespaced all 205 bare command references. Plugin files are Claude's instructions — whatever pattern is in the files, Claude reproduces it.

entries.title: Version bump across 30+ files needs automation
entries.body: v0.4.0 bump touched 30+ files. Missed 3 commands (brand, brand-update, changelog) because they were at v0.2.0 not v0.3.0 — the bump script only replaced the most recent version. Future: search ALL version patterns, not just latest.

entries.title: Derive-don't-declare must derive from the MEANING, not a convenient proxy
entries.body: When replacing a hardcoded value with a computed one, the computation must match the label. Wave 0's {{RULE_COUNT}} was first sourced from len(glob('core/rules/*.md')) but labelled 'Auto-loading behavioral rules' — two populations equal today but that silently diverge the moment a rule sets autoload:false. Fixed to count the autoload-filtered set (same population as {{AUTOLOAD_RULES}}). A drift fix that computes the wrong quantity hides drift behind a green check — worse than the original hardcode because no human watches it anymore.

entries.title: A drift guardrail only covers the surface it reads — enumerate the others
entries.body: C1 made runtimes/claude/CLAUDE.md auto-derive, but CLAUDE.md, README.md and CONTRIBUTING.md still hardcoded the count and were synced by hand; the rule-parity suite watched none of them. Before closing a 'fix the drift' task, grep the whole repo for the stale value and either bring every occurrence under the guardrail or explicitly record the uncovered ones. Wave 0 extended rule-parity to assert the count in all three prose surfaces. Partial coverage plus a confident guardrail name ('rule-parity') creates false assurance.

entries.title: Editing a test to pass is legitimate when it moves the assertion to the correct layer and GAINS failure modes
entries.body: rule-parity was repointed from the source template (which now holds a literal {{RULE_COUNT}} placeholder) to the compiled artifact (what ships), plus a new assertion that the placeholder survives in source. The tell that it's honest, not gaming CI: the test ended up with MORE ways to fail, not fewer. In a source->compile->artifact repo, assertions about shipped behavior belong on the artifact; assertions about the mechanism belong on the source.

entries.title: 'CI was green' proves the triggered jobs, not the bumped dependency or untriggered paths
entries.body: Major-version Action bumps (checkout v4->v5, setup-python v5->v6, github-script v7->v8) clear deprecations but carry SemVer breaking changes. A green PR validates only pull_request-triggered jobs; jobs gated to push-on-main, tags, schedule, or release signing run under the new versions for the FIRST time after merge. For first-party simple-usage actions the empirical bump is proportionate, but explicitly watch the first main push and first signed release post-merge (relevant to release.sh / #18).

entries.title: Verify the side effect, never trust the exit code — for ANY outward-facing command
entries.body: Second occurrence of this bug class in ADD's own tooling: F-001 (`claude plugin validate` exits 0 on schema failure) and #18 (`gh release create` exits 0 without publishing). Both were patched locally and independently. Doctrine: for any irreversible/outward command (validate, publish, deploy, release), assert the OBSERVABLE result (page exists, file validates, deploy is live) and treat a missing side effect as hard failure regardless of exit status. CANDIDATE: encode as a rule near core/rules/quality-gates.md (Gate 5) citing F-001 + #18. Paying for the same lesson twice without writing it down means a third time.

entries.title: Static-assertion tests pin STRUCTURE, not BEHAVIOR — and can silently pass when the fix is reverted
entries.body: Wave 1's first regression test grepped release.sh with `grep | head -1`, which latched onto the --dry-run ECHO lines (not the real commands) and passed even with the whole fix deleted. For a 'command succeeds without doing the thing' bug, a string-presence test reintroduces the exact false-green it's meant to guard. Fix: behavioral harness (mock git/gh/python3 on PATH, run the script, assert exit codes) + a MUTATION CHECK proving the test fails when the fix is removed. If a regression test can't fail, it isn't one.

entries.title: Always run a mutation check on a new regression test
entries.body: The cheapest way to prove a regression guard has teeth: revert the fix (or mutate the guarded code) and confirm the test goes red, then restore. Wave 0's strengthened rule-parity and Wave 1's release test were both validated this way; Wave 1's FIRST test would have been caught immediately by this step (it stayed green when the fix was stripped).

entries.title: Some fixes can only be validated in production — name the first real run as the acceptance test
entries.body: The #18 fix cannot be exercised in CI (CI never pushes a signed tag or calls gh release create). Honest confidence path: treat the next real release (v0.9.6) as Wave 1's acceptance test, and judge success from the new 'published and verified' line + a manual `gh release view`, NEVER from exit 0 (the habit we're retiring). The printed recovery command makes a first-run failure a soft landing. Record outcome in the handoff.

entries.title: A high-false-positive security control is a liability, not a weak asset
entries.body: ADD's unicode-tag-block injection detector ran at 'critical' severity with a ~100% false-positive rate (652/652 sampled events benign, 0 real attacks) for months — a broken byte character-class regex matching almost any multibyte UTF-8. Worse than no detector: it habituates humans and the system to treat output as noise, so a real hit is indistinguishable and dismissed. Treat false-positive rate as a first-class health metric; a detector that never fires correctly is security theater. Fixed in Wave 2 with an evidence-derived precise regex.

entries.title: Committing an append-only audit/telemetry trail to git is what makes its bugs invisible
entries.body: The 6990-line injection-events.jsonl lived in version control. A file that large and noisy becomes scenery — reviewers scroll past it every diff — so the evidence (every event was a benign em-dash) sat in plain sight yet unread. Detectability and hygiene were the same problem. Runtime audit/telemetry trails belong gitignored and local. Fixed: gitignore .add/security/ + untrack.

entries.title: A security-pattern fix is only as good as its tests' NEGATIVE and BOUNDARY coverage
entries.body: The D3 regex fix initially passed because fixtures exercised only the middle of the U+E0000-E007F range (one third-byte branch, neither boundary) and the negative control was pure ASCII — containing none of the multibyte bytes that caused the bug. The green suite could NOT detect a revert. Closed by adding benign-multibyte.json (em-dash/arrow/box/CJK/emoji) asserted to NOT fire, mutation-verified to go red on the old regex. For detectors, the proof-of-fix MUST reproduce the old failure mode. Reinforces L-041.

entries.title: Re-derive a spec's premises from the live system before implementing; risk is dominated, not averaged, across a batch
entries.body: D3's spec claimed 33 events; reality was ~690 — the cohesion review caught this before code, and the re-derivation is what surfaced the 652 false positives. Trusting the document over the running system means implementing the wrong thing correctly. Separately: batching one critical security fix with three trivial items into one wave/commit let it ride the credibility of 'all 15 suites pass' when 14 were irrelevant — isolate highest-risk items for scrutiny proportional to THEIR blast radius.

entries.title: A security self-scan can have the exact bug it exists to catch — validate the detector, not just the content
entries.body: ADD's new skill self-scan (D3-P2) shipped with a (?m)-prefixed pattern handed to grep -E, which rejects it as invalid (rc=2); file_matches treated rc=2 as 'no match', so the most common documented payload (new-instructions-heading, HIGH) silently never gated — the precise 'check that doesn't check' failure the scan exists to prevent. Independent verification caught it post-commit (my own probe happened to use a casing that masked it). Fix: validate every pattern is live at startup and WARN loudly if grep rejects it; mutation-test that the gate fires on EACH engine path (ERE, (?m), byte-mode). Reinforces L-039/L-040/L-041.

entries.title: Whole-file allowlists in a security scan are blind spots; over-broad ones manufacture risk
entries.body: The self-scan's first allowlist exempted 5 whole files — but 3 tripped nothing (pure blind spots) and the 2 real ones were auto-loaded behavioral content, so a critical payload planted in them would be BOTH invisible to the gate and injected into the agent. Fix: precise per-(file,pattern) waivers derived from a no-waiver scan, waiving only the exact patterns a file legitimately documents. An allowlist entry that waives more than what actually fires is a latent hole.

entries.title: Independent per-wave verification catches what implementer self-testing structurally cannot
entries.body: Across all waves the fresh adversarial verifier found real defects the implementer's own green tests missed: Wave 1's false-confidence string-match test, Wave 2's missing negative regression fixture, Wave 3's silently-disabled (?m) pattern. The implementer tests what they thought to test; the verifier tests the documented/boundary cases and reasons structurally. Keep the separate-verifier-per-wave discipline — it is not ceremony, it pays out every time.

entries.title: Maturity gating must be physical, not behavioral, to save tokens
entries.body: The maturity-loader rule suppressed dormant-rule OBEDIENCE but every rule was still @-imported — a POC project paid ~13k tokens/session for rules it was told to ignore. v0.9.9 moved rule injection to a SessionStart hook (load-rules.sh) that reads maturity.level and injects only active rules (~70% POC reduction). Pattern: any 'ignore this content' instruction is a token bug; the content should not be loaded at all.

entries.title: Generated-output substitution must cover every copied tree, not just the primary path
entries.body: compile.py applied codex_substitute only to skill bodies + references; rules/knowledge/templates/lib/TOMLs shipped raw ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT} paths and 286 /add: Claude-namespace refs into the Codex dist. Fix: copy_tree takes a transform callable applied uniformly (v0.9.8). Pattern: when a compiler has per-runtime substitutions, audit EVERY emit path — the leak hides in the 'verbatim copy' branches. Same class as the autoload-predicate divergence (two emitters, opposite defaults for a missing key → require the key explicitly and share one predicate).

entries.title: [agent-retro] Wave-1 parallel dispatch (#23/#25/#27): file-ownership contracts held; adapter-registry gap was the one integration break
entries.body: 3 parallel implementers with strict disjoint file ownership; zero clobbers, both TDD tracks delivered RED-first evidence. Two integration findings: (1) new-skill work MUST include the runtime adapter registry — doctor lacked a runtimes/codex/skill-policy.yaml entry (agent correctly couldn't touch runtimes/), so compile failed AND its partial dist/ made two unrelated installer suites red — misleading cascade; integrator should pre-add policy entries when commissioning a new skill, and treat post-compile-failure test reds as suspect. (2) Prose file paths in skill bodies are load-bearing: the F-002 path suite greps ~/.codex/... references out of installed skill text, so even an example path in prose must resolve — doctor's D-STALE example broke it; reworded. Also: agents flagged out-of-ownership defects (stale --quick error string) instead of fixing — contract worked as designed.

entries.title: [agent-retro] Wave-2 (#28): payload-vs-behavior test design paid off; shell dialect matters for sourced libs
entries.body: Single agent, serial after #27 as planned. It handled the dist-not-yet-recompiled chicken-and-egg correctly by testing installer BEHAVIOR against a simulated prefixed payload instead of asserting on stale dist contents — pattern to keep for changes where source and generated output move together. Post-integration dogfood on the real ~/.codex: legacy cleanup, manifest, and all five doctor checks green. One footgun found: doctor-checks.sh is bash-only; sourcing it from zsh silently breaks sha256 detection and floods false 'user-modified' results — the skill invokes it via bash so users are safe, but the library could guard with a shell check (candidate hardening).

entries.title: [agent-retro] Visual-regen pair (infographic + overview report): design-system-preservation briefs worked; SVG geometry contract held
entries.body: Two parallel agents regenerated docs/infographic.svg and reports/add-overview.html around the v0.11 story. Both preserved the existing design systems as briefed and self-verified; orchestrator re-verified independently (XML parse + viewBox/rect parity + no style/script for GitHub; HTML parse + stale-claim greps) — all clean. Wins to repeat: passing the historical-measurement-labels-stay-as-is rule prevented false re-stamping of the v0.9.11 token chart; requiring agents to state deliberate omissions surfaced good judgment calls (telemetry left out of 'provable installs' because it doesn't emit). Report agent also fixed latent content bugs unprompted (fictional 'Deployer' sub-agent card, '5 engagement modes' contradiction) — regeneration is also an audit.
